evals.name: research-write-review-topology-choice
evals.prompt: I'm building an AI system that needs to research a topic, write a report, and then review it for accuracy. Should I use one agent with multiple stages, or separate agents? If separate, what collaboration pattern — peer review, a manager agent, or something else?
evals.expected_output: Recommends single agent with role switching OR Proposer-Reviewer peer pattern with the reviewer having access to external verification tools (not just re-reading). Explains the new-information criterion: a reviewer re-reading the same text adds no signal. Identifies that a manager is overkill for 3 sequential stages. Recommends shared context (trajectory inheritance) if stages are tightly coupled.
expectations: Applies the new-information criterion to evaluate whether a reviewer agent adds value
Identifies that a reviewer re-reading the same text without external tools adds no new signal
Recommends a specific topology (peer Proposer-Reviewer or single-agent with role switching) with justification
Explains when shared vs non-shared context is appropriate for this pipeline
Addresses the YAGNI gate — whether multi-agent is justified at all for this task

evals.name: reviewer-always-approves-no-improvement
evals.prompt: I have a Proposer-Reviewer setup for generating marketing copy, but the reviewer just says 'looks good' every time and doesn't actually improve anything. What's wrong with my multi-agent design?
evals.expected_output: Diagnoses the root cause: the reviewer has no new information — it's re-reading the same text the proposer generated, so it has no signal to improve on. Recommends giving the reviewer access to external verification (e.g., brand guidelines check, A/B test data, audience persona tool). May also identify cognitive surrender as a loop failure mode. Recommends a verifier grounded in real observations rather than model self-assessment.
expectations: Correctly diagnoses the root cause as the reviewer lacking new information (not just 'bad prompting')
Explains the new-information criterion specifically in the context of the Proposer-Reviewer pattern
Recommends concrete ways to give the reviewer access to new information (external tools, metrics, brand guidelines, etc.)
Mentions cognitive surrender or lazy fake-done as a related failure mode
Does NOT recommend just 'improving the reviewer prompt' as the primary fix

evals.name: concurrent-agents-overwriting-cascading-bugs
evals.prompt: Our multi-agent coding system has 4 agents working on different files simultaneously. They keep overwriting each other's changes and sometimes one agent's bug gets propagated and 'confirmed' by the others. How do we fix this?
evals.expected_output: Addresses two distinct failure modes: (1) concurrency conflicts — recommends working copy isolation (Git branches/worktrees per agent, merge after completion) or optimistic locking; (2) cascading amplification — recommends cross-validation from an independent agent that hasn't seen prior output, plus external validation (tests, compilers) as chain breakers. Should not conflate the two problems.
expectations: Identifies concurrency conflicts and cascading amplification as two distinct failure modes
Recommends working copy isolation (Git branches or worktrees per agent) for the concurrency problem
Recommends external validation (tests, compilers) as chain breakers for the cascading amplification problem
Recommends cross-validation from an independent agent that hasn't seen prior output
Addresses both problems rather than only one
